objectives: Analyze performance characteristics of openrouter_api Rust client
Identify bottlenecks in streaming, retry, cache, serde, and connection pooling
important_facts: 10ms sleep removed from streaming - confirmed absent
Semaphore permits=10 acquired/released in same scope - no backpressure
reqwest::Client cloned per API method call (cheap Arc clone, acceptable)
HeaderMap cloned per retry iteration in closure
serde_json::to_value used in streaming path (intermediate allocation)
Cache has no max-size bound - unbounded growth risk
Token estimation uses len()/4 heuristic - inaccurate but fast
No connection pool tuning (reqwest defaults: ~100 idle, 90s timeout)
ChatCompletionRequest has 27 fields, many Option - large stack size
LinesCodec MAX_LINE_LENGTH=64KB reasonable but redundant check after codec
MCP client serializes request to String for size check then re-serializes via .json()
risks_issues: Semaphore backpressure is a no-op: HIGH impact on memory under load
std::sync::Mutex on cache in async context: MEDIUM, potential deadlock
Double serialization in MCP client: LOW, minor CPU waste
No connection pool configuration: LOW, reqwest defaults are reasonable

iteration.summary: Completed comprehensive performance analysis of the openrouter_api Rust client. Identified 8 optimization opportunities ranked by impact. The semaphore backpressure in streaming is the most critical finding - it provides zero actual backpressure because permits are acquired and released within the same loop iteration. The cache uses std::sync::Mutex in async context which can block the tokio runtime. The streaming path does an unnecessary serde_json::to_value intermediate allocation. Token counting is a fast heuristic with acceptable accuracy tradeoff.
next_actions: Implement fixes for top-priority issues if requested
Add benchmarks with criterion for hot paths
Replace std::sync::Mutex with tokio::sync::Mutex in ProvidersApi cache
